title: Quiz: Qubit Readout Mechanisms
description: Test your understanding of dispersive readout, measurement fidelity, and quantum amplification

questions.question: In dispersive readout, what physical quantity encodes the qubit state information?
questions.explanation: In dispersive readout, the qubit state shifts the resonator frequency by +/- chi. By probing the resonator, we can infer the qubit state from this frequency shift without directly measuring the qubit energy.

questions.question: What is the dispersive shift chi in terms of coupling g and detuning Delta?
questions.explanation: The dispersive shift is chi = g^2/Delta (with modifications for transmon anharmonicity). This comes from second-order perturbation theory in the small parameter g/Delta.

questions.question: For high-fidelity readout, which condition on chi and kappa (resonator linewidth) is desired?

options.text: chi << kappa (shift much smaller than linewidth)
chi >> kappa (shift much larger than linewidth)

questions.explanation: For good state discrimination, chi >> kappa is desired. This ensures the resonator responses for |0> and |1> are well-separated (more than a linewidth apart), making them easily distinguishable.

questions.question: A quantum-limited amplifier adds zero noise to the signal.
questions.explanation: False! A quantum-limited amplifier adds the minimum noise allowed by quantum mechanics - equivalent to half a photon per mode. This is the Heisenberg limit and cannot be surpassed by any amplifier.

questions.question: What is the main trade-off in increasing the measurement strength (photon number) in dispersive readout?

options.text: Higher photon number improves SNR but causes more measurement-induced dephasing
Higher photon number reduces the dispersive shift
Higher photon number makes the resonator frequency unstable
There is no trade-off; more photons is always better

questions.explanation: More measurement photons improve the signal-to-noise ratio but also cause measurement-induced dephasing and potential state transitions. The optimal photon number balances these competing effects.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a Purcell filter in the readout chain?

options.text: To block qubit decay through the resonator while allowing readout signals
To convert microwave signals to optical
To cool the resonator to lower temperatures

questions.explanation: A Purcell filter is a bandpass filter that suppresses the resonator density of states at the qubit frequency (reducing Purcell decay and improving T1) while passing signals at the readout frequency.

questions.question: Which factors affect single-shot readout fidelity? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: All these factors affect readout fidelity: (a) chi determines state separation, (b) amplifier noise affects SNR, (c) T1 decay during measurement causes errors, and (d) kappa affects both measurement speed and state separation.

questions.question: In the IQ plane representation of readout, what do the 'blobs' for |0> and |1> represent?

options.text: The statistical distribution of measurement outcomes for each state
The real and imaginary parts of the qubit wavefunction

questions.explanation: The blobs in the IQ plane represent the statistical distribution of measured (I, Q) values when the qubit is prepared in state |0> or |1>. The separation between blob centers determines distinguishability, and blob size represents measurement noise.
